reasoning: The Federal Reserve is currently operating at a net loss because the interest rate it pays on reserves and reverse repos is substantially higher than the yield it earns on its SOMA portfolio. These losses do not require immediate funding but instead accumulate as a "deferred asset," which is recorded as a negative liability under "Earnings Remittances Due to the U.S. Treasury" (the series in question). According to the FRED data, this series has been monotonically decreasing (getting more negative) by roughly $1 billion to $1.5 billion per week over the entire available 1-year history. Since the question asks whether the value on future dates will be *greater* than the baseline on 2025-08-03, the answer is near-certainly no for the near term. Even if the Fed's target rate is cut enough for it to return to profitability by late 2025 or early 2026, those profits would first merely narrow the weekly losses or slowly rebuild the deferred asset. Since it will have accrued billions in additional negative value between August and any hypothetical breakeven point, it is incredibly unlikely that this value would climb back above the August 2025 baseline by January 2026. Therefore, the probability of an increase is extremely low. Following instructions, uncertainty slightly increases with the forecast horizon.
